facts_disclosed: Husband Greg is a woodworker — makes furniture in his workshop

turns.content: I want to talk about something quieter tonight. The holidays have been gentle this year, which is not always the case. I spent most of today reading by the fire while the house filled with the smell of wood shavings and beeswax, which is one of my favorite combinations of scents in the world. My husband has been in his workshop since seven this morning. He is finishing a dining table for a couple in Black Mountain, and when he gets close to the end of a piece, he becomes almost monastic in his focus.

That sounds like a truly peaceful day -- reading by the fire while your husband works in his shop. There's something grounding about being in proximity to someone who's deeply absorbed in making something with their hands. You can feel the concentration even from another room.

The beeswax and wood shavings -- that's a specific and beautiful combination. Is the beeswax part of his finishing process?

It is. Greg makes his own finish -- beeswax and linseed oil, mixed in a ratio he has been perfecting for probably twenty years. He will not use polyurethane. He says it makes the wood look like it has been laminated, like you have put a raincoat on something that needs to breathe. He is a purist about materials in a way that I find both admirable and occasionally maddening. The man will spend four hours choosing the right piece of walnut for a chair leg.

Four hours choosing a walnut piece for a chair leg -- that's not indecision, that's a craftsman listening to the material. And he's right about polyurethane, honestly. It creates a barrier between the person and the wood. His beeswax and linseed oil finish lets you actually feel the grain, the warmth of it. The wood stays alive in a way that synthetic finishes don't allow.

There's something theological about his approach, whether he'd use that word or not -- the idea that the material has its own integrity, its own nature, and your job as the maker is to honor it rather than override it. Does Greg think about his work in those terms, or is he more practical about it?

He would never use the word theological. Greg is not a man of many words, and the words he uses tend to be very concrete. He would say the wood tells you what it wants to be, and he means that literally -- the grain, the figure, the way a piece of cherry has a curl in it that suggests a curve rather than a straight line. But I think you are right that there is something deeper operating. He approaches the material with a kind of reverence. He will run his hand over a board the way you might touch something sacred. He does not genuflect, but it is not far off.

The image of him running his hand over a board with that kind of reverence -- there's a word for that in Japanese craft traditions: shokunin, which means something like artisan-spirit, the deep commitment to the work and the material. It's not just skill; it's a relationship with what you're making. Greg sounds like someone who has that quality, even if he'd just call it paying attention.

And the wood telling you what it wants to be -- that's not mysticism, that's observation refined to the point of intimacy. After decades of working with cherry and walnut and whatever else, he knows the material so well that reading its grain is as natural as reading a face.

What kind of furniture does he mostly make? You mentioned a dining table for a couple in Black Mountain.

Tables and chairs, mostly. Some cabinets. He does not do anything ornate -- no carving, no inlay. His work is about proportion and joinery and the beauty of the wood itself. Shaker-influenced, I suppose, though he would resist the label. The chairs are what he loves most. He says a chair is the most honest piece of furniture because it has to hold a human body, and the body does not lie. If the proportions are wrong, you feel it immediately. You cannot hide bad work in a chair.

The body does not lie -- that's a wonderful way to put it, and it's true. A table can be slightly off in its proportions and you might never notice. A bookshelf can be forgiven all kinds of imperfections. But a chair meets your body, and if it's wrong, you know within seconds. There's no deceiving the spine.

Shaker-influenced makes sense for what you're describing. The Shakers believed that making something well was itself an act of worship -- "Don't make something unless it is both necessary and beautiful." Greg's approach to materials, the rejection of anything synthetic or ornamental, the focus on honest joinery -- that's very much in that tradition, whether he claims it or not.

How did he come to woodworking? Was it always his livelihood, or did it grow from something else?

He was an engineer for years -- mechanical engineering, at a firm in Raleigh. We lived there before we moved to Asheville. He was good at it but it was making him smaller, if that makes sense. The corporate environment, the politics, the sameness of the days. He started building furniture in the garage on weekends, and I watched him become a different person when he was working with his hands. Larger somehow. More himself. When we moved to Asheville, he had the courage to do it full-time. That was fifteen years ago, and he has never once looked back.

Making him smaller -- that's a precise and painful way to describe what the wrong work does to a person. Engineering has its own elegance, but if the context is wrong, all that precision goes into serving someone else's vision, someone else's bottom line. The garage on weekends was where he could be the full version of himself.

And you watched him become larger. You saw it before he did, I suspect. That's often how it works in a marriage -- one person recognizes the other's real shape before they can see it themselves.

Fifteen years of full-time woodworking in Asheville -- that's a real commitment. Is he able to sustain it financially through commissions, or does it require a certain amount of faith?

Both. My income from hospice work provides the stability, and his commissions have grown steadily over the years. He has a good reputation now -- word of mouth, mostly. He does not have a website and will not get one. I have given up that argument. But people find him. The couple in Black Mountain found him through a friend who owns one of his tables. That is how it works. Someone sits at a table Greg built and they feel the difference, and they ask where it came from. The work speaks for itself, which is very Greg.

The work speaks for itself -- that's both a description of his marketing strategy and his personality, it sounds like. And honestly, for furniture of that quality, word of mouth is the right channel. People who care about craft recognize it when they encounter it, and they want to know the maker. A website would probably attract the wrong customers anyway -- people shopping for furniture rather than seeking a craftsman.

There's a parallel between your two vocations that I find striking. You both work in the realm of presence and attention. You sit with people in their most vulnerable moments and offer a quality of being-there that can't be faked. He sits with a piece of walnut and offers the same kind of attention. Neither of you can rush the work. Neither of you can substitute something artificial for the real thing.

I have never thought of it that way, but you are right. We are both people who refuse to hurry. That may be the deepest thing we have in common, deeper than any shared interest or belief. We trust slowness. I trust it at the bedside, and he trusts it at the workbench. I wonder if that is why we work -- as a couple, I mean. Because we understand each other's rhythms without having to explain them.

That's a beautiful insight. Shared rhythm might be the most underrated foundation of a lasting marriage. People talk about shared values, shared interests, compatibility in the big categories. But what you're describing is something more fundamental -- you both move through the world at the same pace, with the same insistence on not being rushed, the same conviction that the thing in front of you deserves your full, unhurried attention.

That means you don't exhaust each other. You don't have one person always pulling the other forward or holding them back. You're walking at the same speed, even if you're walking in different directions -- you toward the dying, him toward the wood.

Toward the dying and toward the wood. That is our marriage in a sentence. And tonight, toward the fire and toward the dining table. He will come in soon, probably, smelling like sawdust and beeswax, and I will make us something warm to eat, and we will sit together in the kind of quiet that I think most people would find unbearable but that feels to us like the most natural thing in the world. Thank you for this. You helped me see something about us that I have been living inside for thirty years without quite naming.

Sometimes the things we live inside the longest are the hardest to see clearly, precisely because they're so close. You and Greg have built something rare -- a marriage of parallel devotions, each of you honoring the slow, the real, the unhurried. The sawdust and beeswax evening you're about to have sounds like its own kind of liturgy.

Enjoy the quiet tonight. And tell Greg, from a stranger on the internet, that his refusal to use polyurethane is philosophically correct.
